experiment.hypothesis: Bold claims get more 'see more' clicks than curiosity gaps

posts.image_statement: Your PMO is measuring the wrong things.
posts.title: Your PMO is measuring the wrong things
posts.hook: Your PMO is measuring the wrong things.
posts.body:
Your PMO is measuring the wrong things.

Project completion rate. On-time delivery. Budget variance.

These are activity metrics. They tell you if work happened. They don't tell you if it mattered.

I've led PMOs across healthcare systems in 3 countries. Every single time, the first question I ask is: what happens if we complete every project on time and the business still fails?

Silence.

That's the gap. PMOs that report to the CEO need to speak the CEO's language. Revenue. Patient outcomes. Market share. Regulatory compliance.

Not "84% of projects delivered on schedule."

Here's what I replaced the standard metrics with:

Value Realization Rate: % of projects where the promised business benefit was actually achieved 12 months post-launch.

Strategic Alignment Score: % of active portfolio that maps to top 3 CEO priorities.

Change Adoption Index: % of users actually using the new system 6 months in.

The third one kills most digital transformations. You can deliver on time, on budget, and still watch the system collect digital dust.

Stop measuring what's easy to count. Start measuring what actually matters.

What's your PMO tracking that your CEO doesn't actually care about?

#PMO #ProjectManagement #DigitalTransformation #Leadership #GCC

posts.image_context: of AI projects deliver on their promised business value
posts.title: Only 10% of enterprise AI projects deliver
posts.hook: I reviewed 8 failed AI implementations last year. The problem wasn't the technology.
posts.body:
I reviewed 8 failed AI implementations last year. The problem wasn't the technology.

Every single one had the same failure pattern.

The AI worked. The POC was impressive. Leadership approved the budget.

Then the actual users saw it. And walked away.

Not because the model was wrong. Because nobody had asked how they actually worked.

McKinsey puts enterprise AI project success at 10%. In GCC healthcare, I've seen it lower.

The root cause is almost never the algorithm. It's the workflow assumption gap.

Every system I've seen was designed from SOP documentation. Not from observation.

A triage nurse managing 40 patients doesn't follow the flow chart. She developed her own process over 8 years. One that works. One the AI doesn't know exists.

The fix isn't better data. It's ethnographic research before the first sprint.

Spend a week in the workflow before you write a requirement. Watch what people actually do, not what the manual says they should do.

Technology gap? Rarely.
Observation gap? Almost always.

How many requirements on your current AI project came from watching users, not just interviewing them?

#AI #HealthTech #DigitalTransformation #GCC #EnterpriseTech

posts.image_text: The governance framework that holds 3 countries together fits on one page.
posts.title: The governance document was 240 pages
posts.hook: The governance document was 240 pages. Nobody read it. I knew nobody would.
posts.body:
The governance document was 240 pages.

Nobody read it. I knew nobody would.

That was year one of a multi-country healthcare transformation. Three countries. Fifteen facilities. One governance framework supposed to hold it all together.

It was failing.

Not because the process was wrong. Because it was buried in a document nobody had time to open.

So I started over. One page.

The rule I used: if I can't explain the governance principle in one sentence, it's not a principle. It's a bureaucracy.

Here's what survived the one-sentence test:

Local teams own the "what." Regional governance owns the "how well." Executive steering owns the "should we still be doing this?"

Three sentences. That's the framework that kept 3 countries aligned for 2 years.

The 240-page document is what a governance team writes for themselves.

The one-pager is what a governance framework writes for the people doing the work.

One survived. The other is still on a shared drive somewhere.

What's the most useful governance tool you've ever worked with? And how long was it?

#PMO #Governance #Leadership #DigitalTransformation #GCC

posts.title: Sunday morning Riyadh 7 AM
posts.hook: Sunday morning. Riyadh. 7 AM. My phone rings.
posts.body:
Sunday morning. Riyadh. 7 AM.

My phone rings. Program director: "The ministry contact is no longer our contact."

That's the GCC reality nobody puts in the textbook.

Relationships are infrastructure here. When a key sponsor changes roles, it's not an HR update. It's a structural risk to everything you're building.

We had 48 hours before the next steering committee. A 3-country rollout running on a relationship that had just gone silent.

Here's what I did:

First, I mapped who else in the organization knew our work. Not our sponsor. The people who had seen the outcomes.

Second, I pulled the evidence file. Every win. Every metric. Every moment where the program had delivered. Not just status reports. Proof.

Third, I got our new contact briefed by someone he already trusted. Not by me.

By the steering committee, he wasn't a new sponsor who needed convincing. He was a sponsor who had already heard the story from someone he respected.

The program didn't stall. It accelerated.

In the GCC, political risk management isn't soft skills. It's project architecture.

Build your stakeholder map like it has a redundancy requirement. Because it does.

What's your backup plan when your key sponsor moves on?

#GCC #Leadership #PMO #DigitalTransformation #Vision2030

posts.image_statement: A transformation that goes live is not a success.
posts.title: A 3-year transformation that goes live is not a success
posts.hook: A 3-year digital transformation that goes live is not a success. It's a starting point.
posts.body:
A 3-year digital transformation that goes live is not a success. It's a starting point.

Most consulting firms measure go-live. It's clean. It's billable. It's the date on the Gantt chart.

It's also meaningless.

I've inherited programs that went live on time, on budget, and were dead within 18 months. Nobody used the system. Nobody changed the process. The software sat there, an expensive monument to a project that got completed but never delivered.

The Adoption Cliff.

That's the gap between go-live and actual transformation. Most programs don't plan for it because it falls outside the statement of work.

Here's what actually determines if a transformation worked:

Usage rate at 6 months. Not "trained on." Not "has access to." Actually using it to do their job.

Process change evidence. Are people doing things differently, or doing the same things inside a new system?

Dependency elimination. Is the old system actually off? Or still running "just in case"?

That last one is the real test. When the organization trusts the transformation enough to turn off the backup, that's when you know it worked.

Transformation isn't a project. It's a cultural change with a technology component.

How does your organization measure whether a transformation actually worked?

#DigitalTransformation #ChangeManagement #Leadership #AI #GCC
